title: All 8 of Hens and Chicks' companions tell the beginner to plant a full-sun succulent BENEATH a 1 m canopy
Hardy Hibiscus (a marsh perennial the library rates 'high' water) is paired with Chives and Sweet Alyssum, which rot in permanently moist soil
Hellebore -> Summersweet: a well-drained, lime-loving plant paired with an acid bog shrub that suckers into a colony
Foliage-only subjects (Eastern Hemlock, Holly Fern) produce reasons that state no benefit at all
The shared `hibiscus` genus row is written for Rose of Sharon, so native Hardy Hibiscus is scored non-native with only 'some' pollinator value
Honeylocust is told to pair with Trumpet Vine (Campsis radicans), praised as a native pollinator plant with no aggressive-spreader flag or caution
"Honeysuckle" is a bare genus entry (Lonicera) with contradictory traits, so the app recommends a plant that may be Japanese honeysuckle, a listed noxious weed
Maiden Grass (Miscanthus sinensis) is recommended and praised for the seed heads that are its invasion vector, with no spreader flag
Ice Plant (a full-sun succulent) is told to grow beneath a canopy in 7 of its 8 pairings; a 1 m Spirea is called "a canopy to plant beneath"
Bearded Iris is told to plant beneath an Arborvitae, which guarantees rhizome rot and no bloom
Because the subject is a climber, a 3 m Lilac is described as "for the front of the border"
Pampas Grass recommended to Jasmine and praised for the plumes that make it invasive
Butterfly Bush offered as a companion to Italian Cypress and Juniper with no invasiveness caution
Joe Pye Weed paired with Tulip Tree: a 20 m forest tree recommended as a bed companion for a full-sun, wet-soil perennial
Japanese Maple paired with Cardinal Flower: sharp-drainage tree on the same watering schedule as a wet-soil obligate
White-flowered Jasmine described as yellow in six of its eight companion reasons
Japanese Forest Grass credited with seed heads that stand through winter, though the library gives it no bloom at all
Larkspur is told to plant Pampas Grass and is sold on the plumes, the exact organ that makes it a listed invasive
Lilac is told a suckering Campsis radicans is 'height without the footprint' - it will strangle the lilac
Lady's Mantle is handed three running evergreen mats (Vinca minor, Pachysandra, Ajuga) with the spread warning silently switched off
Ligularia, a bog-side plant watered 'high', is paired with two plants whose top killer is wet soil
Lamium maculatum is missing the spreader flag, so a running mat is recommended over a fern and over a short-lived seeder
Colchicum is sent to the front of the border on a height that only measures its leafless autumn flower
Lisianthus -> Pampas Grass: a Cal-IPC-rated invasive recommended, and praised for the plumes that make it invasive
Maidenhair Fern -> Northern Sea Oats: a prolific self-seeder offered as the top companion to a delicate fern, because chasmanthium is missing its spreader flag
Maiden Grass -> Trumpet Vine: reason sells 'height without the footprint' for a plant the library itself flags as an aggressive spreader
Littleleaf Linden -> Butterfly Bush: a Washington Class B noxious weed recommended, uncautioned, and praised for its pollinator value
Layering clause calls a 2 m shrub a ground carpet under any tree subject
Lisianthus -> Gardenia: an acid-soil, constantly-moist shrub paired with an alkaline-soil, rot-prone annual on one watering line
Monkshood's top recommendations are dry-soil Mediterranean/alpine plants (creeping sedum, rock cress, thyme, sea thrift, lavender): opposite soil-moisture regimes
Monkshood + Thyme: the app tells a beginner to carpet a harvested culinary herb under one of the most poisonous plants in the garden, with no caution
Pampas Grass recommended to Moonflower in zones 9-11, praised for the seed heads that are its invasion vector, and called a front-of-border plant at 2.5 m
Mint's spreader warning is misattributed: "this one spreads" reads as the companion, and mint gets no warning at all beside Bugleweed or Azalea
Sweet Potato Vine and Basil, both with no bloom in the library, are told to "offer pollinator forage" (genus trait overrides the foliage check)
Mandevilla + Agave: a fed, evenly moist tropical vine paired with a succulent that rots on that watering schedule
Pampas Grass (Cortaderia selloana) is recommended to Nasturtium and Oregano and praised for the very seed plumes that make it invasive
Ostrich Fern -> Mint: two of the most aggressive runners in the moist-shade palette, recommended together with no benefit stated and no caution
Obedient Plant -> Blue Oat Grass: the caution 'this one spreads' lands on the innocent clumping grass on the companion card
Northern Sea Oats is treated as an ORANGE-flowered plant; four of its eight reasons build a colour scheme that does not exist
New Dawn -> Maiden Grass sells the seed heads of a grass the library itself annotates as invasive, and the caution is silently dropped because a Rose is a 'robust' category
Osteospermum's TOP companion is Joe Pye Weed: a wet-meadow perennial as the partner for a drainage-critical bedding daisy it will also overshadow
Pampas Grass's white plume is printed as "the yellow" in all 8 of its reasons, including "its white flowers settle the yellow" beside white Candytuft
Peony is told to plant itself beneath a 6 m Arborvitae hedge - a full-sun plant under a dense evergreen screen will never bloom
Tulip Tree (20 m x 12 m forest tree) is recommended as a bed companion for a 1.5 m perennial and a 4 m vine, with no mention of its size
Peony's top companion is a wet-meadow plant, and the water conflict is printed as agreement: "Shares full sun and moderate to high water"
Parsley's whole companion set is ranked on "both offer pollinator forage" - for a plant the library gives no bloom period at all
Periwinkle (Vinca minor) is told to carpet beneath shrubs with no spread warning at all - the shrub companions are exempted from the caution
Portulaca is told to grow "beneath" seven taller neighbours, but its flowers only open in direct sun
Maiden Grass (Miscanthus sinensis) is recommended to Potentilla with no caution, praised for the seed heads that make it invasive
Pampas Grass is recommended in zones 8-10 with no invasive caution, and its seed plumes are sold as a benefit
Agave is credited with "leafing out to hide the ripening bulb foliage" of Ranunculus - it does no such thing, and the pair conflicts on water
Garden Phlox's top-ranked companion (80.6) is Sea Thrift, which rots in the rich moist soil Garden Phlox demands
Red Maple and Douglas Fir are called "both native plants", but their native ranges do not overlap anywhere in North America
Lantana camara is recommended to Rosemary and Rock Cress and praised as a pollinator plant, with no invasiveness or toxicity caution
Rhododendron -> Lily of the Valley tells beginners to "carpet the ground beneath" a shallow-rooted shrub with a running, toxic colonizer, and the engine's own spreader warning is silently dropped
River Birch (water: high) -> Boxwood, with the reason claiming they "share ... moderate to high water" - boxwood dies of Phytophthora root rot in a soil kept that wet
Royal Fern (a bog fern, water: high) -> Hellebore, sold as sharing "moderate to high water" - hellebores rot in permanently wet ground
Rock Cress -> Mullein (and 4 more) calls a 1.0-1.2 m herbaceous plant "a canopy to plant beneath" for a sun-demanding alpine mat
Rose of Sharon -> Tulip Tree: a full-sun shrub is offered a 20 m forest tree "a canopy to plant beneath"
Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) is recommended to Sage and Sea Thrift, praised as a "strong pollinator plant", with no invasiveness caution
Pampas Grass (and Miscanthus) recommended for the exact trait that makes them invasive: "its seed heads stand through winter"
Sea Thrift -> Garden Phlox (score 86.9, its 2nd-best pick) is a soil and moisture conflict the low/moderate water band hides
"a canopy to plant beneath" tells beginners to plant full-sun plants in shade: Fan Flower under a Lilac and under a Witch Hazel
Serviceberry -> Tulip Tree offers a 20 m forest tree as a garden companion, and the reason never mentions its size
Scilla -> Blue Oat Grass: the bulb-cover reason is false and contradicts itself in the same sentence
"A canopy to plant beneath" is emitted from height ratio alone, with no light check: Shasta Daisy is told to go under a 6 m crabapple
Pampas Grass recommended to Star of Bethlehem and praised for the seed heads, which are exactly what makes it invasive
Butterfly Bush is the top-scoring companion for Snow-in-Summer with no invasiveness caution
Japanese Forest Grass is credited with winter seed heads "for structure and the birds" although its own library record has no bloom months at all
Rosemary paired with Spiderwort: a Mediterranean subshrub that rots in a moist, part-sun border, on one watering schedule
Colchicum's library height (0.15 m) is the flower only, so the engine front-rows a bulb whose defining problem is 0.4 m of collapsing spring foliage
Pampas Grass is recommended to three subjects and praised for the seed plumes that make it a Cal-IPC "High" invasive
Sweet Potato Vine is credited with "pollinator forage" in 6 of its 8 reasons, but it is a foliage plant that never flowers
Sugar Maple -> Trumpet Vine: a suckering woody vine recommended for a tree, with the spreader warning silently deleted
Lavender and Rosemary are sold as sharing a watering schedule with thirsty bedding plants ("Shares full sun and low to moderate water")
Sugar Maple's entire companion list is full-sun, moisture-loving plants - the engine matched the tree's own light need, not the shade and root mat it creates
Summersweet -> Bugleweed: an aggressive stoloniferous groundcover sold on its carpeting habit, with the caution suppressed
Trumpet Vine is presented as a canopy with lilac, viburnum and witch hazel as its "understory" - Campsis will strangle all three
Thyme's second-best companion tells the beginner to plant it beneath a 1.5 m sunflower - a shaded, damp grave for a drought mat
Sweet Woodruff is paired with Pachysandra: two rhizomatous mats in the same 0.2 m tier, and the engine's 'let them fight' rule silences every warning
Northern Sea Oats is missing spreader:true, so it is recommended (top slot) to Trillium, a slow, disturbance-intolerant woodland native it will seed over
Sweet Potato Vine is credited with "pollinator forage" although the library gives it no bloom period at all
"This one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time" names the wrong plant - it is the subject that spreads, but every other clause in the sentence describes the companion
Vinca -> Pampas Grass: an invasive grass recommended in exactly its invasive zones, praised for the seed heads that make it invasive
Viburnum -> Trumpet Vine: a flagged aggressive spreader is recommended to sit beside a 2 m shrub, and sold as 'height without the footprint'
Weeping Willow -> Joe Pye Weed (and sunflower, canna, hydrangea): full-sun perennials told to 'carpet the ground beneath' a 12 m willow
Turtlehead -> Asiatic Lily: a bog-margin perennial paired with a bulb that rots in soil that stays wet
Wild Ginger -> Japanese Forest Grass: the reason credits Hakonechloa with winter seed heads it does not have
Verbena -> Feather Reed Grass: the reason promises 'orange against purple' from a green-and-wheat grass
Trumpet Vine (Campsis radicans) is sold to beginners as "height without the footprint" beside Winterberry and Witch Hazel - it is a root-suckering thug that will engulf both shrubs
Pampas Grass is recommended to Wisteria and Yarrow and praised for the wind-borne seed plumes that make it a listed invasive across its own hardiness range
Winterberry gets 8 companions and not one of them is the male Ilex it must have to set a single berry - the same-genus hard filter guarantees the correct companion can never be shown

Winterberry's bloom window is filed as October-December in red - that is the berry display, not the flowers - so the highest-weighted scoring factor for all 8 of its pairings is computed against a bloom that does not exist

The height-layering clause tells beginners to carpet the ground beneath big shrubs and trees with full-sun, sharp-drainage plants: Sea Thrift under Witch Hazel (its top companion, 74.2), Calibrachoa under a swamp-loving Winterberry, Zinnia under a 6 m Crabapple

The bulb-cover clause is fired at evergreens, producing a self-contradictory sentence: Blue Oat Grass "leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back" and, in the same breath, is "evergreen"
Pampas Grass (Cortaderia selloana) is recommended in ~191 lists, praised for the plumes that ARE the invasion vector, and carries no spreader flag at all
Maiden Grass (Miscanthus sinensis): 68 lists, and the reason praises "its seed heads stand through winter" - the seed heads are exactly how it invades

Butterfly Bush: the engine recommends the fertile species Buddleia davidii (illegal to sell in WA and OR) in 42 lists and praises it as a "strong pollinator plant", while the two seed-sterile cultivars in the same group can never be recommended

Periwinkle (Vinca minor) is recommended under azaleas and praised for being an evergreen mat that will "carpet the ground beneath" - the exact behaviour that makes it a woodland invasive, and a poor root partner for a shallow-rooted azalea

"Wisteria" is filed as a bare genus with no species, no spreader flag, and is recommended with the clause "climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint" - false for a tree-girdling, root-suckering vine

"Honeysuckle" is filed as bare genus Lonicera, ranks as a top-scoring companion (84.6 for aster), and is praised as a "strong pollinator plant" - the name a beginner will buy is one of the worst invasives in the eastern US
The caution clause is structurally unreachable for a spreader recommended under a shrub, tree, rose or climber - which is why 0 of the 72 rows in this lens carry any warning
Siberian Squill (Scilla siberica) is recommended in ~10 lists and praised for its ability to "carpet the ground beneath", with no spreader flag and no caution
The "witch-hazel" archetype (bare genus Hamamelis) is tagged native but carries the Feb-Mar bloom of the East-Asian hybrid H. x intermedia; 18 pairings inherit both errors
"Jacob's ladder" (bare genus Polemonium) is called native from the genus flag, but the 50 cm blue May-June garden plant is the Eurasian Polemonium caeruleum
The default "columbine" and "columbine-white" are bare-genus Aquilegia in purple and white - the colours of the European A. vulgaris, which the library elsewhere explicitly demotes as non-native
"Wild ginger" carries a hard native TAG on a genus-only record (Asarum); the wild ginger in commerce is very often the European A. europaeum
The default "viburnum" is an unpinned, fragrant, white spring shrub - the profile of the Asian V. carlesii - yet it is the one viburnum in the library still flagged native
'Green Giant' arborvitae is filed as Thuja plicata but is the hybrid Thuja standishii x plicata (Japanese parent); it is called a native plant in 6 pairings
Campsis x tagliabuana 'Madame Galen' is a hybrid with the Chinese C. grandiflora, but inherits the genus native flag and is sold to the user as a native
Agave (unpinned genus, zones 8-11) is flagged native and paired with California poppy as "both are native plants" - two plants native to nothing the reader's garden shares
Every Tropical Hibiscus card tells you to carpet Echeveria under it; the Echeveria rots
Library data: Tropical Hibiscus is filed water=moderate; it is a high-water plant
Agave and Aloe are the fallback companion for zone 10-11 thirsty vines (Mandevilla, Thunbergia, Moonflower, Passiflora): the succulent rots
Agave is credited as a bulb cover and the reason claims it "leafs out" - it is an evergreen succulent and it cannot
Lavender is recommended as the carpet beneath magnolias and a Japanese maple, and as a bedfellow for witch hazel: the lavender dies
Ironweed and Perennial Sunflower list Thyme as their #1 companion, to "carpet the ground beneath" them
Library data: Monkshood is filed water=moderate, which lets it pair with Lavender and be used as a canopy over Creeping Sedum
When water needs differ the reason says "shares ... low to moderate water" and no caution is ever printed
Witch Hazel's top-ranked companion is Lavender, a plant the repo's own data says dies in exactly the soil witch hazel demands
Six magnolias tell the beginner to 'carpet the ground beneath' the tree with lavender
Georgia Peach Pie dianthus is told to plant beneath a witch hazel canopy, the one place its own data says it rots
Star Magnolia's white flowers are described to the reader as 'the orange' (and Gardenia's and Southern Magnolia's as 'the yellow')
The colour clause promises a contrast between two flowers that are never open in the same month
Clematis recommended into an azalea or camellia, whose shallow root plates must stay undisturbed and unlimed
Crown Jewel gardenia is given a 2 m forsythia to sit under, though its own data says it dislikes root competition
An 18 m evergreen Southern Magnolia is sold to a forsythia as 'shares full sun' - the tree is what removes the sun
Pampas grass is recommended 191 times and every single reason praises the seed heads that make it invasive
The spreader caution never fires for a woody neighbour, so trumpet vine is offered to a 0.6 m English boxwood with no warning
Two documented garden thugs get zero caution because the engine decides to 'let them fight': mint is recommended next to bugleweed
The colour clause names the wrong colour in roughly half the reasons that mention colour: black-eyed Susan is called orange, Solomon's Seal is called yellow
"A soft colour beside the white" is attributed to the wrong plant: a blazing orange Kniphofia is called a soft colour
"A canopy to plant beneath" is applied to sunflowers, ferns, alliums and pampas grass: 1,179 of 3,077 canopy clauses have no canopy
A 15 m Colorado blue spruce is offered as a bed companion for garden phlox at exactly the 15.0:1 height cap, with a reason that states no benefit
The layering clause floor makes the canopy sentence unreachable above 8:1, which is what produces 43 of the 44 empty reasons

The height clause is silently suppressed on exactly the most extreme pairings: 15 m Colorado blue spruce is the #2 companion for 1 m garden phlox, and the reason is "Shares full sun and moderate water."
Sun-loving prairie perennials recommended under an 18 m sugar maple, with the reason asserting "Shares full sun"
A 20 m tulip tree is the #2 companion for a 1.4 m annual sunflower, scored 86.5, with no hint that it is a forest tree
"Carpet the ground beneath" is printed for herbaceous plants with no canopy at all: a 1.1 m Allium 'Gladiator' (a bare flower stalk) told to have crocus carpeting beneath it
Spring-ephemeral bulbs are cast as the ground "carpet" in 561 reasons, though the engine's own code documents that bulbs go dormant and cannot cover
"Low at 2.5 m, to carpet the ground beneath it": a 2.5 m Rose of Sharon and a 2 m ninebark are called ground carpets
Ground-sweeping dwarf conifers are treated as canopies: all 8 of Mugo Pine's companions are told to "carpet the ground beneath it", under a plant 1.5 m tall and 2.5 m wide
Echeveria (a succulent) is the #2 companion for Tropical Hibiscus, recommended to carpet the ground beneath a thirsty, heavy-feeding shrub
Dahlia's whole companion list is a zone 8-10 list: half of it (gardenia, calla lily, amaryllis, ranunculus) dies in the zones where dahlias are actually grown
Every "they only overlap in zone N" pairing is, by construction, the one zone where BOTH plants are marginal - and the code comment asserts the opposite
Tropical hibiscus is told to plant creeping phlox and candytuft at its feet - two dry, cool-climate mat plants, in zone 9, the one zone that kills them
Echeveria, a 10 cm full-sun succulent, is told to carpet the ground beneath a 1.5 m sunflower and a 1.5 m switchgrass
Black-eyed Susan vine and Mandevilla, sold everywhere as annual patio vines, get pure zone-10 companion lists: agave and aloe for a zone 6 buyer
Freesia is told to build its bed structure out of two cool-season grasses, in the one zone where those grasses fail
Annual sunflower is credited with holding the bed's winter structure in 172 reasons
Lily of the valley -> Camellia works only in zone 7, and nearly everyone reading the lily-of-the-valley page gardens in zones 3-6 where the camellia is killed
Hosta is filed sun "full-shade" with no bloom window, so America's best-selling shade perennial is recommended to nobody (0 of 1,611 lists)
Coral Bells (Heuchera) has no bloom in the library, yet its own hybrid Heucherella does - the parent is orphaned and its 8 reasons are contentless
Perennial Sunflower (bare genus Helianthus) has no spreader flag, so the #1 companion in the catalog (402 of 1,611 lists) is never once cautioned
Every ornamental-grass genus row in taxonomy.ts is empty, so pampas grass (a listed noxious weed) is recommended 191 times and praised for its plumes with zero caution
Fountain Grass is filed water "low"; it is a medium-water grass, and 112 of its 308 recommendations put it in genuinely xeric beds under the words "shares full sun and low water"
Autumn Crocus (Colchicum) records only its 15 cm flower, so 187 of its 188 recommendations put a plant with 40 cm of coarse spring foliage at the very front of the border
Rosemary's zone floor is one zone too warm (7-10, should be 8-10), and it is the single most-recommended companion in the catalog (419 of 1,611 lists)
Japanese Maple is filed sun "part-shade", which blocks it from all 1,211 full-sun plants and leaves the most-planted specimen tree in America recommended to nobody
Ostrich Fern is recommended Mint "for the front of the border" with no caution: the engine mutes the warning precisely when both plants are thugs
Oregano (an edible herb you pick and eat) is told to plant Autumn Crocus at the front of the border: Colchicum is lethally toxic and its leaves are mistaken for wild garlic
Bee Balm, the most mildew-prone perennial in the catalog, is told to plant itself beneath a 6 m Crabapple canopy
"A canopy to plant beneath" is said of Pampas Grass, a solid 2.5 m clump with razor-serrated leaves: there is no beneath, and reaching in cuts you
Lily of the Valley is recommended "for the front of the border": red berries at toddler height, cardiac glycosides in every part, and a coloniser that will eat the bed
Northern Sea Oats is not flagged as a spreader although it self-seeds prolifically, so the catalog's 5th most-recommended companion is never cautioned
Basil is filed as a zone 10-11 Herb, so its entire companion list is tropical plants a zone 5 gardener cannot grow
A running groundcover under a shrub is never cautioned: Bugleweed is recommended as a "carpet" under Beautyberry with no mention that it runs into the lawn
